hero.heading: 🏗️ Terraform - Infrastructure as Code Made Simple
hero.subtitle: Define your entire cloud infrastructure in code. Version it, test it, deploy it consistently across clouds.
hero.whyLearn: If you manage cloud infrastructure, Terraform is the industry standard. Used by Netflix, Uber, AWS, Google, and thousands of companies.

storytelling.problem: Manually clicking AWS console to create servers, databases, networks. Mistake? Manual delete and recreate. Scale? Manual setup for each environment. Nightmare.
storytelling.solution: Terraform lets you describe infrastructure in code. Apply once → all resources created. Change code → resources update. Delete code → resources destroyed. Automatic and consistent.
storytelling.analogy: Terraform is like a blueprint for building. You draw blueprint (write HCL), contractor builds it (terraform apply). Change blueprint → rebuild. Delete blueprint → demolish.
storytelling.realWorldExample: Uber uses Terraform to manage millions of cloud resources across hundreds of services. GitOps workflow: code review, approve, auto-deploy.
storytelling.deepDive: Terraform is declarative. You describe desired state, Terraform handles implementation details. Works with AWS, GCP, Azure, Kubernetes, GitHub, and 500+ providers.

architectureFlow.title: Write HCL Code
architectureFlow.description: Define infrastructure in Terraform files (main.tf, variables.tf)
architectureFlow.details: Describe resources: EC2 instances, RDS databases, VPCs, security groups, load balancers, etc.

architectureFlow.title: Plan Changes
architectureFlow.description: Run terraform plan to see what will be created/modified/deleted
architectureFlow.details: Review plan before applying. Catch mistakes before making expensive infrastructure changes.

architectureFlow.title: Apply Changes
architectureFlow.description: Run terraform apply to create/update resources in your cloud account
architectureFlow.details: Terraform manages state. Knows what exists and what needs to change.

architectureFlow.title: Manage State
architectureFlow.description: Terraform state file tracks all resources it created
architectureFlow.details: Store state in remote backend (S3, Terraform Cloud). Team members see same state.

beginner.description: Terraform configuration language. Resources, variables, outputs.
AWS, GCP, Azure, Kubernetes, GitHub. Tells Terraform how to talk to services.
EC2, RDS, S3, VPC. Building blocks of your infrastructure.
Core workflow commands. Initialize, preview, deploy.

intermediate.description: Remote state backends. Locking. Avoid conflicts.
Query existing resources without managing them.
Manage multiple environments (dev, staging, prod) with same code.

advanced.description: Write tests for your infrastructure code.
Detect when cloud resources diverge from code.

commands.whatItDoes: Downloads provider plugins, sets up state management, prepares for terraform operations
commands.realUseCase: First step in any Terraform project. Required after cloning repo or changing backends.
commands.example:
terraform init
Initialized Terraform working directory containing .terraform configuration
commands.commonMistake: Forgetting to run terraform init causes provider not found errors

commands.whatItDoes: Shows what resources will be created, modified, or destroyed
commands.realUseCase: Always run before applying. Catch mistakes that could damage production.
commands.example:
terraform plan
Plan: 3 to add, 0 to change, 0 to destroy
commands.commonMistake: Skipping plan and applying blindly causes unexpected infrastructure changes

commands.description: Create or update infrastructure based on Terraform code
commands.whatItDoes: Applies the planned changes to your cloud provider
commands.realUseCase: Deploy infrastructure. Used in CI/CD pipelines. Requires careful review of plan first.
commands.example:
terraform apply
Apply complete! Resources: 3 added, 0 changed, 0 destroyed
commands.commonMistake: Running without reviewing plan first can destroy production resources

commands.description: Delete all infrastructure managed by Terraform
commands.whatItDoes: Destroys all resources defined in Terraform code
commands.realUseCase: Clean up resources when no longer needed. Avoid unnecessary costs.
commands.example:
terraform destroy
Destroy complete! Resources: 3 destroyed
commands.commonMistake: Accidentally destroying production environment. Use prevent_destroy meta-argument for critical resources.

commands.description: List all resources managed by Terraform
commands.whatItDoes: Shows all resources tracked in state file
commands.realUseCase: Understand what Terraform is managing. Debug state issues.
commands.commonMistake: Manually deleting resources without telling Terraform creates state drift

comparisons.title: Terraform vs CloudFormation vs Pulumi

right.name: ☁️ CloudFormation / 🐍 Pulumi
points: CloudFormation is AWS-only but tightly integrated
Pulumi uses real programming languages (Python, Go)
Pulumi more powerful but steeper learning curve

comparisons.whenToUse: Use Terraform for multi-cloud or broad provider support. Use CloudFormation if AWS-only. Use Pulumi if you want programmatic control.

beginnerMistakes.title: Storing State File Locally
beginnerMistakes.description: State file in git means passwords and secrets exposed. No team collaboration. Corrupted state breaks everything.
beginnerMistakes.howToFix: Use remote backend (S3, Terraform Cloud). Enable state locking. Never commit state files.

beginnerMistakes.title: Using 'latest' for Provider Versions
beginnerMistakes.description: Uncontrolled provider upgrades break your infrastructure. Different results for different team members.
beginnerMistakes.howToFix: Pin provider versions in code. Require_version = '~> 5.0' for explicit control.

realWorldUsage.name: Netflix
realWorldUsage.usage: Netflix uses Terraform to manage massive multi-cloud infrastructure across AWS, GCP, and on-prem.

realWorldUsage.name: Uber
realWorldUsage.usage: Uber uses Terraform with GitOps for thousands of microservices infrastructure.

realWorldUsage.name: Airbnb
realWorldUsage.usage: Airbnb manages infrastructure-as-code using Terraform for consistency and auditability.

productionSection.scaling: Use modules to scale infrastructure patterns. Terraform workspaces for multiple environments. Parallelization with -parallelism flag.
productionSection.security: Store state securely (S3 with encryption). Use IAM roles for Terraform service accounts. Vault for secrets management. Plan reviews before apply.
productionSection.monitoring: Track state changes with audit logs. Monitor terraform apply runs. Alert on infrastructure drift. Use policy-as-code (Sentinel).
productionSection.cicd: Store terraform code in Git. Require PR reviews before apply. Auto-plan on PRs. Auto-apply on merge. GitOps workflow.

labs.title: Deploy Your First Server
labs.description: Create an EC2 instance using Terraform

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between terraform plan and terraform apply?
interviewQuestions.answer: Plan shows what changes will be made without making them. Apply executes those changes. Always review plan before apply.
interviewQuestions.example: Run plan first to catch mistakes. Then apply if plan looks good.
